Problem Statement
Today, item setup in the execution workflow requires multiple handoffs between merchants and suppliers. While AEX captures a significant amount of planning and execution data , the item creation process still relies on suppliers to:
• Review pre-populated information
• Manually supplement missing data
• Submit additional attributes required for item creation
• Await merchant approval for critical fields
This results in:
• Manual rework and back-and-forth communication
• Delays in item creation and PO readiness
• Increased risk of data inconsistencies
• Dependency on merchant approvals to validate supplier-submitted changes
• Reduced scalability of execution workflows
In many cases, many item creation critical data already exists within AEX at the time of bridging. Additionally, because suppliers can modify certain fields during item setup, merchant approval steps are required to confirm critical changes adding further delay and operational overhead.
There is also downstream enrichment (such as variant grouping) that is currently either manual or partially automated, despite the necessary inputs already existing within AEX.

Move Fashion Line Planning to AP Tool Assortment List

When the AEX Fashion Line Planning experience was originally built, historical LY and LLY selling data was intended to help merchants make informed Pre-Season assortment decisions by reviewing prior performance, determining how many styles and colors to carry under each fineline, adding those to their current Line Plan in the tool, and then editing TY plan metrics (Pre-Season) or adding incremental styles and colors as needed.

In practice, Fashion merchants rely on enterprise selling history tools such as MerchOne and Scintilla**, outside of AEX Fashion,** to inform high-level assortment breadth, such as how many finelines, styles, and colors they can carry in a season. Those high-level decisions are then shared with Suppliers, Product Development, and Sourcing Partners, and detailed assortment inputs for the season are later provided back to merchants through spreadsheets from Suppliers (primarily National Brands) and internal Product Development and Sourcing partners (primarily Private Label).

Merchants use historical LY and LLY data and/or the “Add Existing” fineline functionality, along with the ability to add styles and colors in AEX Fashion Line Planning, to set up the required fineline, style, and color structure that aligns to these partner inputs and to complete the attributes needed for AEX downstream modules and execution integration, not as the primary source of Pre-Season assortment details.

As a result, Line Planning today requires significant manual consolidation and re-entry of partner inputs, increasing effort and risk of errors, and limiting the ability of AEX Fashion to serve as an integrated starting point for Pre-Season assortment planning.

Lock All Modules After a FL if Bridged

Today a fineline that has a Commit Report generated or has been bridged can still have important edits made in multiple modules. If pack optimization or other critical edits are rerun after the commit/bridge, outputs can differ and cause downstream inconsistency in execution (PO creation, supplier quotes, item creation). The original solution locked pack optimization only and now we must expand locking to more user edits so that the plan that was committed/bridged remains stable and consistent.

Uncontrolled edits after commit or bridge have caused production incidents, rework, and execution failures.

- PD develops proprietary/private brand concepts in OneSource, where merchants cannot see early work.
- Designers and PD teams develop concepts in Centric, also without merchant visibility.
- Merchants operate in AEX or Assortment Planning (AP), lacking visibility into upstream PD/Design intent.

Each tool maintains its own version of "potential items," blocking collaboration, duplicating effort, and preventing downstream services from operating on a single, consistent dataset. This delays merchant decisions, creates redundant concept creation, and prevents SKU-level forecasting until very late in the process.

Problem Statement  Today, item setup in the execution workflow requires multiple handoffs between merchants and suppliers. While AEX captures a significant amount of planning and execution data , the item creation process still relies on suppliers to:  • Review pre-populated information  • Manually supplement missing data  • Submit additional attributes required for item creation  • Await merchant approval for critical fields  This results in:  • Manual rework and back-and-forth communication  • Delays in item creation and PO readiness  • Increased risk of data inconsistencies  • Dependency on merchant approvals to validate supplier-submitted changes  • Reduced scalability of execution workflows  In many cases, many item creation critical data already exists within AEX at the time of bridging. Additionally, because suppliers can modify certain fields during item setup, merchant approval steps are required to confirm critical changes adding further delay and operational overhead.  There is also downstream enrichment (such as variant grouping) that is currently either manual or partially automated, despite the necessary inputs already existing within AEX.

When the AEX Fashion Line Planning experience was originally built, historical LY and LLY selling data was intended to help merchants make informed Pre-Season assortment decisions by reviewing prior performance, determining how many styles and colors to carry under each fineline, adding those to their current Line Plan in the tool, and then editing TY plan metrics (Pre-Season) or adding incremental styles and colors as needed.    In practice, Fashion merchants rely on enterprise selling history tools such as MerchOne and Scintilla**, outside of AEX Fashion,** to inform high-level assortment breadth, such as how many finelines, styles, and colors they can carry in a season. Those high-level decisions are then shared with Suppliers, Product Development, and Sourcing Partners, and detailed assortment inputs for the season are later provided back to merchants through spreadsheets from Suppliers (primarily National Brands) and internal Product Development and Sourcing partners (primarily Private Label).    Merchants use historical LY and LLY data and/or the “Add Existing” fineline functionality, along with the ability to add styles and colors in AEX Fashion Line Planning, to set up the required fineline, style, and color structure that aligns to these partner inputs and to complete the attributes needed for AEX downstream modules and execution integration, not as the primary source of Pre-Season assortment details.    As a result, Line Planning today requires significant manual consolidation and re-entry of partner inputs, increasing effort and risk of errors, and limiting the ability of AEX Fashion to serve as an integrated starting point for Pre-Season assortment planning.
Move Fashion Line Planning to AP Tool Assortment List

Walmart's early-stage product development and evaluation processes are fragmented across multiple systems:  - PD develops proprietary/private brand concepts in OneSource, where merchants cannot see early work.  - Designers and PD teams develop concepts in Centric, also without merchant visibility.  - Merchants operate in AEX or Assortment Planning (AP), lacking visibility into upstream PD/Design intent.  - Suppliers submit concepts via Supplier One or email, creating parallel "shadow repositories."  Each tool maintains its own version of "potential items," blocking collaboration, duplicating effort, and preventing downstream services from operating on a single, consistent dataset. This delays merchant decisions, creates redundant concept creation, and prevents SKU-level forecasting until very late in the process.
Milestone - Full Review of Verticals and Ownership With Proposal

Merchant strategy is currently defined outside of core systems (presentations, spreadsheets, meetings) and manually interpreted by downstream teams. As a result:  Strategy is not consistently enforced.  Design and assortment decisions drift from merchant intent.  Systems reflect outputs but not intent.  Changes in strategy cause rework and confusion.  There is no system-level mechanism to input, version, and execute merchant strategy across OneSource and Centric.

Walmart requires a shared event calendar and lightweight project management system that synchronizes workflows, timelines, and milestones across MerchOne (merchants), OneSource (sourcing/technical), and SupplierOne (suppliers) in order to ensure all users—internal and external—operate on the same sequence of events and critical business milestones tied to PLM, sourcing, and product readiness.
